nativeRange.habitat: Hill streams, river margins, and areas near rapids in clear, fast-flowing water; typically found near the substrate in moderate to strong current

diet.notes: Primarily an algae grazer; one of the few fish reliably documented consuming Black Brush Algae (BBA) and hair algae. Base diet is algae, biofilm, and aufwuchs; supplement with sinking spirulina wafers and blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, peas). High-protein foods should be minimised — a protein-heavy diet tends to reduce motivation to graze algae.

goodWith: Amano shrimp and larger shrimp species
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (cherry, neocaridina — will be eaten or stressed)
red-tail sharks and rainbow sharks (territory conflicts)
Chinese Algae Eater (Gyrinocheilus aymonieri — competitive and aggressive)
Flying Fox (Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus — misidentification risk; can be territorial)

summary: The true Siamese Algae Eater is widely considered the most effective freshwater algae control fish, and is one of the few species reliably documented eating Black Brush Algae. Best kept in groups of 4–6 in a well-oxygenated 150 L+ tank.
careNotes:
IDENTIFICATION: The true SAE (Crossocheilus oblongus sensu trade) is frequently mislabelled and confused with two lookalikes in the trade: the Flying Fox (Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus) and the Chinese Algae Eater (Gyrinocheilus aymonieri). Neither lookalike reliably eats BBA. Key distinguishing feature: on a true SAE the dark lateral stripe extends into the forked caudal fin (both lobes), whereas on Flying Fox and Chinese Algae Eater the stripe stops at the caudal peduncle. Always verify identity before purchasing for BBA control.

TAXONOMY NOTE: The name 'Crossocheilus siamensis' was long used in the hobby and is now a junior synonym. The currently accepted name per FishBase and modern taxonomy is C. oblongus. SeriouslyFish has noted that C. oblongus as originally described from Java may be a distinct taxon from what is typically traded; the commonly traded fish may actually be C. langei or related congeners. For practical purposes, most hobbyist and trade sources use C. oblongus as the accepted name for the commonly kept SAE, and this entry follows that convention.

GROUPING: Though peaceful with other species, intraspecific semi-aggression has been reported in groups of 2–3 adults; keeping 4 or more tends to distribute any chasing behaviour. SeriouslyFish recommends groups of 6+. Solitary individuals have been described as shy or less active.

FEEDING: To maintain algae-grazing motivation, avoid overfeeding prepared foods. A lean feeding regime allows the fish to naturally graze algae throughout the day. Juveniles are more reliably active algae consumers; adults may reduce grazing activity.

JUMPING: SAEs are active, fast-moving fish known to jump. A tightly-fitting lid is generally advised.

OXYGENATION: Native to fast-flowing, well-oxygenated hill streams. Good filtration and surface agitation are generally recommended; stagnant, low-oxygen conditions should be avoided.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquariums has not been reliably documented. Commercial breeding is reported to require hormone treatment. Sexing is generally difficult until maturity (3–4 years), at which point females tend to be noticeably larger (approximately 20–30% heavier). Spawning behaviour in captivity is rarely observed.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary freshwater fish source. Covered scientific name, tank size (50–55 gal), temperature (68–80°F / 20–27°C), pH (6.0–8.0), adult size (15 cm), diet, temperament, tankmate guidance, and care notes.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked via C. langei page (the fish most commonly traded as SAE per SeriouslyFish's taxonomy review). Confirmed adult size (14–15 cm), temperature (20–26°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (18–268 ppm), minimum tank base dimensions (150×45 cm), BBA consumption, and shoaling group requirement (6+). Also documents the nomenclature ambiguity around C. oblongus vs C. langei vs C. atrilimes in the trade.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cyprinidae / Cypriniformes), native range (Thailand to Indonesia), adult size (16 cm SL), temperature (24–26°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (5–20 dH). Temperature range is narrower than other sources (likely reflects wild stream conditions).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked lifespan (up to 10 years), adult size (~15 cm / 6 in), temperature (23.9–26.1°C / 75–79°F), pH (6.5–7.0), hardness (5–20 dH), and breeding (hormone-only in commercial farms). Their minimum tank recommendation (20 gal / 76 L) is assessed as undersized for a group and not used here.

fish.breedingNotes: Not documented as breeding in home aquariums. Commercial breeding relies on hormone injection. Sexing is impractical until maturity (~3–4 years); mature females tend to be larger than males.
